title: Business Models That Reward Substance
thesis: Ad-supported platforms will tend to optimize for engagement; alternative business models—subscriptions, patronage, public funding, cooperatives, and reputation systems—can align platform incentives with substance.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Advertising-funded platforms optimize for time-on-site and ad impressions, which favors engagement over quality.

evidence.snippet: Aggregation Theory describes how platforms commoditize suppliers, centralize demand, and monetize through advertising, creating incentives to maximize attention.

counterevidence.summary: Ad-supported platforms can and do invest in quality when it serves advertiser interests, such as brand safety and premium inventory; engagement is not the only metric they track.

claims.claim: Subscriptions align incentives with user satisfaction and retention.

evidence.snippet: Patreon enables creators to earn recurring revenue directly from fans, shifting the incentive from maximizing ad impressions to sustaining subscriber satisfaction.
The Reuters Institute Digital News Report tracks the growth of subscription and membership models for news and creative content.

counterevidence.summary: Subscription models can exclude low-income users, favor established voices, and still optimize for retention through sensational or addictive content.

claims.claim: Public funding and philanthropy can support content that has civic value but limited commercial appeal.

evidence.snippet: NPR's public radio funding model combines federal, state, and private support to produce journalism and programming with civic rather than purely commercial goals.

counterevidence.summary: Public funding can introduce political pressure, bureaucratic inefficiency, and reduced responsiveness to audience preferences.

claims.claim: Cooperatives and reputation economies can reward contributions that the market undervalues.

evidence.snippet: Cooperatives are owned and governed by members, aligning organizational incentives with member benefit rather than external extraction.

counterevidence.summary: Cooperatives and reputation systems face governance challenges, free-rider problems, and status competition that can undermine quality and sustainability.

agentInstructions: Treat the four claims as distinct: the ad-supported default, subscriptions, public funding/philanthropy, and cooperatives/reputation economies.
When discussing business models, keep the comparison structural rather than prescriptive; no single model is presented as the universal solution.
Cite sources by their IDs and URLs from the artifact; do not generalize beyond the public evidence provided.
Flag that the argument is about incentive alignment and model diversity, not a statistical measurement of platform effects.
Route empirical questions about advertising revenue in India to the Who Profits? article and design questions to Engagement Is a Design Choice.
